summary:
The Jamestown Settlement (1607 AD) was the first permanent English colony in North America, established on May 14, 1607 on a tidal island in the James River in Virginia by the Virginia Company of London, marking the beginning of the English colonial presence that would ultimately become the United States. The settlement's early years were catastrophic: of the 104 original settlers, most were gentlemen unprepared for manual labor, and the colony nearly perished repeatedly through starvation, disease (the brackish water of the chosen site was unhealthy), conflict with the Powhatan Confederacy, and internal governance failures. During the 'Starving Time' of 1609–1610, the population fell from approximately 500 to 60, with survivors reportedly resorting to cannibalism (confirmed by recent archaeological evidence). The colony was repeatedly rescued from abandonment — by new supplies, new settlers, and the leadership of John Smith.

Jamestown's transformation from a failing commercial venture into a viable colony came through two developments: the cultivation of tobacco, introduced by John Rolfe around 1612 from a Caribbean strain that proved commercially viable in English markets, providing an export crop that made the colony profitable and created insatiable demand for agricultural labor; and the headright system (1618), which granted 50 acres to any person who paid for another's passage, driving immigration and establishing a plantation economy. The labor demand created by tobacco eventually drove the importation of the first Africans to English North America (1619), planting the seeds of American slavery. The dissolution of the Virginia Company and the conversion of Virginia to a royal colony (1624), combined with the House of Burgesses (founded 1619 — the first representative legislative assembly in the New World), established the template for English colonial governance in North America.
